title: SCRUM Fundamentals Mock Exam
subtitle: Scrum Certification Practice Set
description: Imported Scrum mock-exam questions based on the provided PDF content.

questions.q: What is the recommended size of a Scrum Team?
questions.explain: SBOK recommends a Scrum Team size of six members plus or minus three, which gives a practical range of three to nine.
questions.deep: That range keeps communication manageable while still allowing enough breadth of skills to deliver increments.

questions.q: What is produced at the end of each Sprint?
questions.explain: Each Sprint should produce a potentially shippable product increment.
questions.deep: The increment is evidence that Scrum is delivery-focused. Progress is measured in working product, not just activity.

questions.q: Which event demonstrates completed work to stakeholders?
questions.explain: The Sprint Review is used to inspect the increment with stakeholders and gather feedback.
questions.deep: This event closes the feedback loop between delivery and business expectations.

questions.q: Who decides how the work will be performed?
questions.explain: The Scrum Team is self-organizing and decides how to accomplish the work.
questions.deep: Scrum separates the what from the how. The Product Owner orders value, while the team determines execution.

questions.q: What is the purpose of Sprint Planning?
questions.explain: Sprint Planning defines the Sprint Goal and the work the team commits to for the Sprint.
questions.deep: It aligns backlog selection with capacity and creates a shared plan for the Sprint.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the Sprint Retrospective?
questions.explain: The Retrospective is focused on inspecting the team's process and identifying improvements.
questions.deep: It exists so Scrum can improve not just the product, but also the system that produces the product.

questions.q: What does Scrum emphasize for managing complex projects?
questions.explain: Scrum is built on empirical process control using transparency, inspection, and adaptation.
questions.deep: Complex work cannot be fully predicted upfront, so Scrum relies on learning from real outcomes instead.

questions.q: Which Scrum artifact tracks work remaining in a Sprint?
questions.explain: A Burndown Chart shows remaining work against time during the Sprint.
questions.deep: It provides a quick visual signal of whether the Sprint is on track.

questions.q: Which Scrum value emphasizes honest and transparent communication?
questions.explain: Openness encourages transparency and clear communication inside and around the team.
questions.deep: Without openness, inspection and adaptation break down because the team cannot see reality clearly.

questions.q: Who prioritizes Product Backlog items?
questions.explain: The Product Owner orders backlog items according to value and other product needs.
questions.deep: This keeps prioritization accountable to a single role rather than becoming a negotiation by committee.

questions.q: Which Scrum meeting is limited to 15 minutes?
questions.explain: The Daily Scrum is a 15-minute time-boxed event.
questions.deep: The short time-box forces focus on coordination rather than drifting into problem-solving sessions.

questions.q: Scrum teams should be:
questions.explain: Scrum expects teams to organize their own work and decide how best to deliver it.
questions.deep: Self-organization improves ownership, adaptability, and local decision-making.

questions.q: What is the Definition of Done?
questions.explain: The Definition of Done is a shared standard for what qualifies as complete.
questions.deep: It protects quality by making completion criteria explicit rather than assumed.

questions.q: What does Scrum encourage for project delivery?
questions.explain: Scrum promotes iterative and incremental delivery so value can be released early and often.
questions.deep: Smaller increments reduce risk and improve learning from stakeholder feedback.

questions.q: Who represents stakeholder interests in Scrum?
questions.explain: The Product Owner represents customer and stakeholder interests.
questions.deep: That role connects business priorities to backlog ordering and product direction.

questions.q: What does a Sprint Backlog contain?
questions.explain: The Sprint Backlog contains the selected work and tasks for the current Sprint.
questions.deep: It is the team's working plan for turning chosen backlog items into a usable increment.

questions.q: What principle allows Scrum teams to adapt quickly?
questions.explain: Inspection and adaptation let teams detect reality early and respond quickly.
questions.deep: This is one of the core mechanisms behind empirical process control.

questions.q: Scrum promotes what type of development?
questions.explain: Scrum delivers work in short iterative cycles that add value incrementally.
questions.deep: That structure reduces risk and supports continuous learning.

questions.q: What is the main goal of Scrum?
questions.explain: Scrum focuses on maximizing business value through frequent delivery and feedback.
questions.deep: Its purpose is not ceremony for its own sake, but faster value realization under uncertainty.

questions.q: Who attends the Sprint Review?
opts: Only the Product Owner and developers
questions.explain: The Sprint Review includes the Scrum Team and relevant stakeholders.
questions.deep: This event exists so feedback reaches the product decisions directly.

questions.q: Which artifact evolves throughout the project?
questions.explain: The Product Backlog is continuously refined, reprioritized, and updated as the project progresses.
questions.deep: It remains dynamic because knowledge, priorities, and market needs change over time.

questions.q: What is velocity in Scrum?
opts: Amount of work completed in a Sprint
Cycle time of one backlog item
questions.explain: Velocity measures how much work a team completes in a Sprint.
questions.deep: It is useful for forecasting, but only when treated as a planning signal rather than a performance target.

questions.q: What type of teams does Scrum require?
questions.explain: Scrum Teams are expected to be cross-functional so they can deliver increments without handing work off across silos.
questions.deep: Cross-functionality reduces dependencies and speeds up delivery.

questions.q: What happens to unfinished work at the end of a Sprint?
opts: It is returned to the Product Backlog
It becomes part of the Definition of Done
questions.explain: Unfinished work goes back to the Product Backlog for re-evaluation and reprioritization.
questions.deep: Scrum avoids pretending incomplete work is done. The backlog remains the source of truth.

questions.q: What is the purpose of backlog refinement?
questions.explain: Backlog refinement improves backlog readiness by clarifying, splitting, and estimating items.
questions.deep: A refined backlog makes Sprint Planning faster and improves the team's ability to make realistic commitments.

questions.q: Which communication style does Scrum encourage most strongly?
questions.explain: Scrum emphasizes direct communication because it reduces delay and misunderstanding.
questions.deep: The closer the feedback loop, the faster the team can coordinate and adapt.

questions.q: Who ensures Scrum practices are followed and facilitates the framework?
questions.explain: The Scrum Master facilitates Scrum and helps the team follow effective Scrum practices.
questions.deep: This is a coaching and enabling role, not a command role.

questions.q: What does transparency ensure in Scrum?
questions.explain: Transparency ensures everyone can see the real state of work, progress, and problems.
questions.deep: Inspection is only useful when the thing being inspected is visible and truthful.

questions.q: Which meeting occurs after the Sprint Review?
questions.explain: The Sprint Retrospective follows the Sprint Review and focuses on process improvement.
questions.deep: The Review inspects the product outcome, while the Retrospective inspects the team's way of working.

questions.q: What is the key output of the Sprint Review?
questions.explain: The Sprint Review generates stakeholder feedback that can influence backlog priorities and product direction.
questions.deep: That feedback is one of Scrum's main engines for adaptation.

questions.q: What is a key advantage of Scrum?
questions.explain: Scrum's short cycles make it easier to respond quickly when requirements or conditions change.
questions.deep: Rather than trying to eliminate change, Scrum is designed to absorb it.

questions.q: What most directly helps ensure product quality in Scrum?
questions.explain: The Definition of Done establishes the quality bar for completed work.
questions.deep: Without a shared quality standard, teams can claim progress while delivering inconsistent outputs.

questions.q: What is time-boxing in Scrum?
questions.explain: Time-boxing means Scrum events and activities have fixed maximum durations.
questions.deep: It creates urgency, focus, and predictability while preventing endless meetings or analysis.

questions.q: What is the core Scrum philosophy?
opts: Delay delivery until all requirements are complete
questions.explain: Scrum centers on iterative delivery of value with continuous feedback and adaptation.
questions.deep: Its core assumption is that learning during delivery improves both the product and the process.
